title: Post-Trial Assurance & Close-out (REQ020) absent from user stories; archive capacity must be provisioned
reasoning: REQ020 (Post-Trial Assurance & Close-out) is not covered by any user story in the original requirements (requirements-221fbff5.csv), yet CAP 722A §23 mandates 10-year evidence retention for investigation and regulatory audit. This creates a capacity gap: archive infrastructure (immutable storage, WORM volumes, checksums, disaster recovery) must be specified and provisioned before trial operations begin. Without this specification, evidence may be lost to bit rot, mishandling, or inadequate backup. Regulatory non-compliance risk is severe (CAP 722A violation).
recommendation: Create user story REQ020-bis: 'As a CAA investigations officer, I want immutable 10-year evidence archives so that incident investigations and regulatory audits can access complete trial records.' Provision archive capacity incrementally: (1) Define archive schema (what constitutes a 'complete case record'), (2) Implement WORM storage (S3 with versioning disabled + checksums, or enterprise SAN with WORM volumes), (3) Establish backup/DR for archive tier (off-site replicas, encrypted), (4) Design archive access protocols (incident investigation workflow, audit trail of who accessed what), (5) Implement lessons-learned aggregation (cross-case trend analysis, REQ020 post-trial analysis).

title: Security Classification Handling (REQ018) absent; SECRET/STRAP tier infrastructure not scoped
feature: classification tier provisioning and access control
reasoning: REQ018 (Security Classification Handling) is absent from user stories, yet CAA/MAA trials involving MOD autonomy assurance (GCAP/Tempest, advanced swarm autonomy) require classified evidence handling per CAP 722H and JSP 440 (Information Security Manual). Current Workbench design assumes OFFICIAL tier (cloud-hosted, high availability). SECRET tier requires on-premises air-gapped infrastructure: separate DB replicas, isolated network, restricted access (MOD personnel only), immutable audit trails per access. STRAP tier (Special Tactical Requirement) adds further complexity (physical compartmentalization, need-to-know controls). Without capacity specification for classified tiers, MOD trials cannot proceed. Projected 10% of submissions by 2028 will be SECRET tier (GCAP/Tempest advanced autonomy); 5% STRAP (tactical swarm operations). This is a major cost and operational complexity driver (~4200 GBP/month estimated).
recommendation: (1) Create user story REQ018-bis: 'As a CAA/MOD liaison, I want classified evidence segregation per JSP 440 so that MOD autonomy assurance trials can share classified airworthiness data without compromise.' (2) Establish MOD Data Protection Officer liaison (CAP 722H compliance authority). (3) Design SECRET tier architecture (on-premises, air-gapped, dual-replica for resilience, WORM audit logs). (4) Design STRAP tier access controls (physical compartmentalization, cleared personnel roster, need-to-know matrix). (5) Budget and procure on-premises infrastructure (2026 Q2-Q3 for deployment by Q4 2026, ahead of GCAP/Tempest trials). (6) Establish classification-aware data flows: ingestion procedures, declassification workflows, audit trails.

title: Swarm-Aware SORA Evidence Capacity Gap (DeFOSPAM Dorothy-1 + Vibe SwarmAwareSORAEvidence)
feature: SORA assessment and swarm-specific evidence audit
reasoning: DeFOSPAM finding Dorothy-1 flagged 'swarm-aware' as undefined in REQ002. Vibe analysis (SwarmAwareSORAEvidence scale) quantified the gap: status 15% of submissions contain swarm-specific SORA evidence, tolerable 80%, regulatory minimum (JARUS SORA v2.5 Appendix C) 5 distinct items per aircraft for complex swarms. This creates a SORA assessment capacity bottleneck: (1) Current evidence is insufficient; operators will submit legacy SORA v2.0 packs lacking swarm-specific failure-mode analysis. (2) SORA assessors must conduct extended audits (METER-003: Swarm-Specific Risk Evidence Inventory Audit, ~5-10 hours per case vs. 3-5 hours for single-aircraft). (3) Rework cycles will increase (operators resubmit evidence after SORA gate rejection). Estimated impact: 15-25 extra hours per year by 2028 across inspector pool (5-8 FTE), plus 20-30% longer authorization timelines for swarm trials.
recommendation: (1) Publish JARUS SORA v2.5 swarm-specific evidence templates (6 months pre-trial): ConOps sections addressing swarm interdependencies, failure-cascade evidence, emergent-behavior testing, de-confliction algorithm validation. (2) Host webinars for applicants (Q1 2026): SORA swarm-aware evidence requirements, case studies, common pitfalls. (3) Deploy METER-003 (Swarm-Specific Risk Evidence Inventory Audit) as formalized SORA process: risk-item extraction checklist, swarm-specific tag taxonomy, evidence traceability log. (4) Allocate 2-3 FTE dedicated swarm SORA assessor roles (recruit by 2026 Q3). (5) Establish lessons-learned feedback loop: after each swarm authorization, update guidance based on assessor and applicant experience. Target: increase status (15%) to 60% by 2027 Q2 (via guidance + training), 80% by 2028 Q4 (via mature practice).

title: Phased Trial Build-Up (REQ016) drives sustained multi-month submission and review load
feature: multi-stage application review and phased gates
reasoning: REQ016 (Phased Trial Build-Up) specifies that evidence builds progressively through gates, allowing reversibility. This design choice creates sustained capacity demand (long-running submissions in-progress) vs. batch processing. Vibe benchmark PackCompleteness indicates 28% first-submission failure rate; under phased-gate design, applicants will resubmit evidence multiple times (per-gate rework). Impact: (1) Concurrent active submissions remain high (10-20 simultaneously), each occupying evidence-store space and requiring reviewer bandwidth. (2) Review panels meet more frequently (5-15 per quarter vs. 2-3 if upfront completeness were stricter). (3) Database indexes must optimize for partial-evidence queries (what evidence is available for gate N vs. gate N+1?). Capacity model assumes sustained load; underestimating will cause database contention and reviewer burnout.
recommendation: (1) Establish phased-gate SLA per REQ016: gate 1 (application intake + completeness check): 2 weeks turnaround; gate 2 (SORA + safety-case review): 4 weeks; gate 3 (crew training + ConOps validation): 2 weeks; gate 4 (joint panel authorization): 3-6 weeks. (2) Publish gate-specific checklists (Alexa's scales from vibe analysis): PackCompleteness (gate 1), SwarmAwareSORAEvidence (gate 2), ConOpsCompleteness (gate 3), AuthorizationReadiness (gate 4). (3) Implement per-gate evidence staging (Workbench data model): evidence required for gate N is archived; evidence for gate N+1 is in active review; older gates offloaded to cold storage. (4) Size database for 20 concurrent active submissions (peak); partition by gate to reduce index cardinality. (5) Monitor gate turnaround times; establish escalation procedures if gates begin to queue (bottleneck detection).

title: ML Assurance Case (REQ009) adds inference-intensive evidence review workload
feature: AI/ML assurance case review and explainability evidence analysis
reasoning: REQ009 (ML Assurance Case) specifies AMLAS/SACE evidence + frozen-model manifest + explainability evidence. This is new content type not previously handled: (1) Evidence volume: AMLAS case study ~50-200 MB (training data summaries, model architecture docs, test reports, confidence scores); (2) Review complexity: ML assurance requires specialist review (AI/ML safety engineer); current CAA inspector pool has ~2-3 ML-qualified personnel (insufficient for ramp-up); (3) Evidence analysis: explainability outputs (saliency maps, decision trees) and training-data statistics require computational tools (ML model introspection libraries, data profiling). Estimated impact: 3-5 extra hours per case (AMLAS pilot cohort ~5-10 cases/year by 2028), plus capital cost for ML evidence analysis tools (GPUs, libraries, licenses).
recommendation: (1) Recruit 2-3 AI/ML safety engineers to CAA inspector pool (by 2026 Q3); consider contract specialists from University of Sheffield CAA centre or Bristol University. (2) Establish AMLAS/SACE evidence checklist (per Vibe scale SwarmAwareSORAEvidence as precedent): minimum 20 evidence items per ML component, covering training-data provenance, model validation, robustness testing, failure modes, explainability. (3) Procure ML evidence analysis tools: MLflow (model registry), SHAP (explainability), Weights & Biases (training experiment tracking) as cloud services (integration with Workbench evidence store). (4) Create AI/ML assurance user story: 'As a CAA AI/ML safety engineer, I want interpretable ML evidence and frozen-model manifests so that I can assess the safety and airworthiness of autonomous flight systems.' (5) Target: by 2027 Q4, 50% of advanced autonomy submissions (GCAP/Tempest, Project Alvina cohort 2) include AMLAS evidence; by 2028 Q4, 100% of submissions with ML components.

title: Submission intake processing capacity must scale to 4-5 submissions/week by 2028
reasoning: Capacity model projects intake growth from 1-2/week (2026 Q2) to 4-5/week by 2028 Q4 (assumes successful Protector RG Mk1 + Project Alvina + GCAP/Tempest rollout). This drives database transaction rate from 25 TPS (baseline) to 100+ TPS peak (concurrent reviews + panel deliberations). Evidence store grows from 5 GB (2026) to 60+ GB (2028). Database indexes must remain performant; traditional RDBMS (PostgreSQL) will require tuning (partitioning by year + case stage, incremental index builds, statistics updates). Peak concurrent users: 60-70 (applicants + inspectors + panelists) by 2028 Q4. Web tier should auto-scale (Kubernetes or equivalent) to handle 50-100 req/sec during peak review seasons.
recommendation: (1) Perform database load test (2026 Q3): validate 100+ TPS sustained throughput with 20 concurrent active submissions, 50 concurrent users. Identify bottlenecks (index scans, full-table scans, locking contention). (2) Implement partitioning strategy: submissions partitioned by year + stage; archive tables partitioned by retention tier. (3) Deploy read replicas (primary + 2x replicas) for high-volume inspector review queries (read-heavy workload); async replication lag <100 ms acceptable. (4) Monitor key metrics: P95 query latency, transaction throughput, index fragmentation. Set alerts for degradation (P95 >500 ms, TPS <80 when expected 100+). (5) Plan database upgrade cycle (2026 Q2: PostgreSQL 15, 2027 Q2: add sharding if growth exceeds single-node capacity).

title: Swarm C2 bandwidth budget must support 10-20 concurrent vehicles, 200+ KB/sec peak
reasoning: Capacity model projects C2 bandwidth per REQ006 (authorized monitored C2): 5-vehicle swarm at 5 Hz telemetry + 1 Hz coordination = 25 msg/sec telemetry + 20 msg/sec coordination = 50 msg/sec nominal = 25-50 KB/sec. Peak scenarios (DAA encounter, high-frequency sensor updates): 10 vehicles at 10 Hz = 200 KB/sec (exceeds LTE single-link capacity ~150 kbps per carrier). For 10-vehicle swarm, must provision 2x LTE links or hybrid (LTE + fixed RF backup). Terrestrial network availability assumption: 99.5% uptime is insufficient for safety-critical C2 (worst-case: link loss during critical maneuver). Link-loss handling (REQ006 link loss survivable) requires onboard autonomy fallback + geofence containment (REQ005). Capacity constraint: remote trial locations (Scottish Highlands, Welsh countryside) may have poor LTE coverage; satellite C2 backup (~400 kbps max) may be only option.
recommendation: (1) Define C2 bandwidth SLA per trial location: urban (LTE primary, 500+ kbps available) vs. rural (LTE + satellite hybrid) vs. remote (satellite primary, 400 kbps guaranteed). (2) Conduct LTE coverage survey (2026 Q2) for planned trial sites (Protector RG Mk1 locations, Project Alvina routes); map coverage gaps; identify satellite providers. (3) Procure dual-modem hardware (each vehicle): primary LTE (4G/5G) + secondary satellite (Inmarsat IDP or Iridium). (4) Establish C2 link monitoring (REQ006 monitored C2): real-time link quality metrics (RSSI, packet loss, latency); crew dashboard alerts if link quality degrades below safety threshold. (5) Define link-loss procedures (REQ011 contingency matrix): <100 ms uplink loss = continue current plan; 100 ms - 5 sec = trigger geofence RTL (return-to-launch); >5 sec = autonomy failsafe (land in-place or descend). (6) Validate with flight test: confirm link-loss detection + failsafe triggering in simulation and low-altitude trials before high-altitude BVLOS.

title: DAA sensor fusion compute latency <500 ms; swarm coordination must scale to 10+ vehicles without exceeding decision-making budget
feature: DAA sensor fusion and encounter de-confliction
reasoning: REQ007 (Detect-and-Avoid Performance) specifies DO-365 / F3442 alignment, implying <500 ms latency budget for de-confliction decisions. Swarm coordination adds complexity: 5 vehicles = 10 pairwise coordination messages per cycle; 10 vehicles = 45 pairwise messages. At 5 Hz coordination rate, 10-vehicle swarm = 225 msg/sec inter-vehicle = 450+ KB/sec if uncompressed. Onboard compute (Jetson Nano, 10 GFLOPS) cannot sustain full-rate fusion for 10 vehicles; must compress (filter out non-critical tracks, batch messages). Latency risk: fusion algorithm takes >200 ms, decision-making <300 ms available, but margin is thin. Network latency (C2 relay of fusion data) adds 50-100 ms; total latency stack >500 ms during adverse conditions (congested airspace, multiple encounters). Safety impact: delayed de-confliction decision = collision risk.
recommendation: (1) Conduct DAA fusion latency analysis (2026 Q2-Q3): measure end-to-end latency (sensor input → decision output) for 5, 7, 10-vehicle swarms in simulation. Identify latency bottlenecks (sensor processing, fusion algorithm, decision logic). (2) Establish latency budget allocation: sensor processing 50 ms, fusion algorithm 150 ms, decision logic 100 ms, network delay 50 ms, margin 50 ms = 500 ms total. (3) Design fusion algorithm with graceful degradation: nominal case (all sensor data available) uses full fusion; degraded case (missed messages, high latency) uses simplified tracks (last-known position + velocity). (4) Implement adaptive message compression: fusion messages include only tracks with delta >threshold (e.g., bearing change >2 degrees). Nominal: 100-200 msg/sec; peak: 225 msg/sec. (5) Deploy on heterogeneous compute (dual-core ARM + GPU or TPU for ML acceleration); validate <500 ms latency in flight test. (6) Establish contingency: if fusion latency exceeds budget, revert to point-defense DAA (each aircraft responsible for own de-confliction; loss of swarm coordinated maneuvers but preserves safety).

title: Clause library versioning and semantic search must support quarterly regulatory updates with <500 ms query latency
feature: clause library and compliance guidance search
reasoning: Clause library is the authoritative source of CAA compliance guidance for applicants and inspectors. Quarterly revision cadence (JARUS SORA v2.5, AMLAS/SACE updates, CAP 722 amendments, ML explainability templates) adds 50-100 MB per revision. Case officers expect sub-500 ms search latency for interactive compliance lookups (What is the current SORA requirement for loss-of-separation risk? What ML explainability evidence is required?). Full-text indexing + semantic embeddings (vector search for similar clauses) requires significant compute. Archive of 5+ years (4 GB cumulative) makes traditional search tools (simple LIKE queries) too slow. Classification tier variants (OFFICIAL + OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE clauses for upper tiers, SECRET clauses for MOD trials) multiply storage and index size.
recommendation: (1) Deploy vector search database (Pinecone, Weaviate, or Elasticsearch with vector plugin, 2026 Q3): index all clause library versions with semantic embeddings (HuggingFace all-MiniLM-L6-v2 or OpenAI embeddings). (2) Establish library versioning strategy: publish release notes for each quarterly update; maintain backward-compatible old-version indices (inspectors may need to cite guidance applicable at time of case opening). (3) Implement caching layer (Redis): cache hot queries (e.g., 'SORA loss-of-separation', 'ML explainability evidence') with 24-hour TTL. (4) Set latency SLA: P95 search latency <500 ms, P99 <1 sec. Monitor via application performance metrics; alert if degraded. (5) Design classification-aware search: OFFICIAL tier searches use public library; OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE tier searches restricted to CAA personnel; SECRET tier searches restricted to MOD liaison. (6) Conduct search UX study (2026 Q4): gather feedback from case officers and inspectors on clause discovery; refine search UI and taxonomy.

title: Inspector pool must scale from 30-40 FTE (2026) to 50-60 FTE (2028); training and onboarding capacity critical
feature: inspector review capacity and SORA assessment
reasoning: Current CAA + MAA inspector pool: ~30-60 active users (estimate). By 2028 Q4, expected ramp: (1) Protector RG Mk1 trials (2026-2027): 1-2 inspectors dedicated; (2) Project Alvina BVLOS cohort (2027-2028): 3-5 dedicated; (3) GCAP/Tempest advanced autonomy (2028+): 5-10 dedicated (including ML safety specialists). Total headcount growth: 30 → 60 FTE. Challenge: swarm-aware SORA assessment (REQ002) and ML assurance (REQ009) require specialist training; CAA currently has <5 JARUS SORA v2.5 certified assessors and <3 ML-qualified engineers. Training pipeline lag: new hires need 3-6 months to reach productivity; 2027-2028 bottleneck likely if recruitment delayed. Workbench must scale to support 60 concurrent users with low latency; current design (2-3 application servers) will be under-capacity by 2027 Q4.
recommendation: (1) Establish CAA/MAA recruitment plan (2026 Q1): identify 20-30 inspector positions to fill by 2027 Q4. (2) Partner with universities for training: University of Sheffield CAA centre (SORA/JARUS specialists), Bristol University (ML safety), Loughborough (human factors). (3) Create inspector training curriculum (6 months intensive): SORA v2.5 swarm-aware assessment, AMLAS/SACE ML assurance, ConOps review, safety-case evaluation. (4) Establish mentorship program (pair new inspectors with experienced reviewers for 3 months). (5) Allocate 2-3 FTE to training delivery and curriculum maintenance. (6) Scale Workbench infrastructure ahead of headcount: upgrade application servers (3 → 5 instances, 2026 Q3); upgrade database replicas (2 → 3, 2026 Q4); add caching layer (2027 Q2). (7) Monitor inspection cycle times (gate turnaround per step): if >SLA, escalate hiring or prioritize training.

title: Panel deliberation audit trail (immutable WORM logs) must survive system failures; recovery capability essential
feature: joint review panel deliberation recording and audit trail
reasoning: Panel deliberation audit trail (REQ012 MOR obligations) is immutable and forensically critical: if a panel decision is later challenged, the audit trail must prove what evidence was reviewed, who decided, and what conditions were set. Current capacity model assumes ~100 KB per action × 15 panels/year × 100 actions/panel = 150 MB/year cumulative audit trail. By 2028, cumulative audit trail 450+ MB. Database replication (normal failover) is insufficient: if primary DB is corrupted, replica may also be corrupted (cascading failure). Must implement write-once storage (WORM) with separate backup: S3 with versioning disabled + MFA delete protection, or dedicated WORM NAS. Disaster recovery test: simulate data loss, confirm audit trail recoverable within <24 hours (operational requirement: cannot delay authorization decisions pending archive recovery).
recommendation: (1) Implement dual-layer audit trail: live transaction log (PostgreSQL WAL, fast recovery) + immutable archive (S3 WORM, long-term forensics). (2) Configure S3 WORM bucket: object lock with retain-until-date (10 years minimum per REQ012), MFA delete protection, separate AWS account from operational systems (air-gapped credentials). (3) Establish backup-to-archive pipeline: every 6 hours, dump audit trail from live DB to S3 WORM; checksums computed and verified. (4) Conduct disaster recovery drill (2026 Q4, 2027 Q4): simulate live DB corruption; confirm recovery from S3 WORM <24 hours; verify audit trail integrity (no missing records). (5) Monitor S3 WORM bucket for compliance (audit trail should only grow, never shrink or change). Alert if delete attempts (MFA delete should block). (6) Plan for long-term preservation: by 2036, convert S3 objects to enterprise archive media (optical discs, tape) for cost efficiency and resilience.
